title: How to Prevent Malaria in Sierra Leone
summary: Malaria is the leading cause of illness and death in Sierra Leone. Learn how to protect yourself and your family.
tips: Sleep under insecticide-treated mosquito nets every night
Use mosquito repellent (DEET-based) on exposed skin at dusk and dawn
Wear long-sleeved clothing and long trousers in the evenings
Eliminate standing water around your home where mosquitoes breed
Take antimalarial prophylaxis if prescribed by your doctor
Seek immediate treatment if you experience fever, chills, or headache
warning: If you have a fever above 38°C, seek medical attention within 24 hours. Malaria can be fatal if untreated.
prevalence: High risk year-round, peak during rainy season (May–October)

title: Staying Safe During Pregnancy
summary: Sierra Leone has one of the highest maternal mortality rates in the world. Prenatal care saves lives.
tips: Register for antenatal care as early as 8 weeks of pregnancy
Attend all scheduled antenatal appointments (at least 8 visits recommended)
Take iron and folic acid supplements as prescribed
Deliver in a health facility with a skilled birth attendant
Sleep under an insecticide-treated net during pregnancy
Get tested for HIV, syphilis, and hepatitis B during pregnancy
Do not take any traditional herbs or medicines without medical advice
warning: Danger signs during pregnancy: heavy bleeding, severe headache, blurred vision, swollen face/hands, reduced baby movements — go to hospital immediately.
prevalence: Sierra Leone has 443 maternal deaths per 100,000 live births (WHO 2023)

title: Preventing Typhoid & Waterborne Diseases
summary: Typhoid fever spreads through contaminated food and water. Prevention is simple and saves lives.
tips: Drink only boiled, bottled, or chlorinated water
Wash hands thoroughly with soap before eating and after using the toilet
Eat only freshly cooked, hot food from trusted sources
Avoid raw vegetables and salads unless you've washed them with clean water
Store food in covered containers to prevent contamination
Dispose of waste properly and keep your environment clean
Get the typhoid vaccine if available in your area
warning: Symptoms: persistent high fever, headache, stomach pain, loss of appetite. Seek treatment within 48 hours to avoid complications.
prevalence: Affects thousands of Sierra Leoneans annually, especially during rainy season

title: Protecting Your Family from Cholera
summary: Cholera is endemic in Sierra Leone, especially near coastal areas. Basic hygiene prevents outbreaks.
tips: Always boil drinking water or use water purification tablets
Wash hands with soap before preparing food, eating, and after toilet use
Use a proper latrine and practice open defecation-free behaviour
Do not buy food from street vendors with poor hygiene
Breastfeed infants exclusively for the first 6 months
Report suspected cholera cases to the nearest health centre immediately
warning: Cholera causes severe diarrhoea and vomiting. Dehydration can kill within hours. Oral rehydration salts (ORS) are life-saving.
prevalence: Sierra Leone experiences regular outbreaks, especially in Western Area and Bo

title: HIV Prevention & Treatment in Sierra Leone
summary: HIV affects about 1.5% of Sierra Leone's adult population. Testing, prevention, and ART treatment save lives.
tips: Get tested for HIV — it's free at government health centres
Use condoms consistently and correctly with every sexual encounter
Know your partner's HIV status before unprotected sex
People living with HIV should start antiretroviral therapy (ART) immediately
Prevent mother-to-child transmission through PMTCT programmes
Do not share needles, syringes, or any sharp instruments
Stigma is the enemy — treat people living with HIV with respect and dignity
warning: HIV has no cure but is manageable with ART. People on ART can live long, healthy lives and cannot transmit the virus when undetectable.
prevalence: Approximately 85,000 Sierra Leoneans are living with HIV (UNAIDS 2023)

title: Preventing Childhood Malnutrition
summary: Malnutrition affects 1 in 3 Sierra Leonean children. Proper feeding in the first 1,000 days is critical.
tips: Exclusively breastfeed your baby for the first 6 months — no water, no formula
Continue breastfeeding alongside complementary foods until age 2
Introduce diverse, nutritious foods at 6 months: vegetables, legumes, eggs, fish
Ensure children receive all routine vaccinations on schedule
De-worm children aged 12–59 months every 6 months
Take your child to regular growth monitoring appointments
Enrol children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in feeding programmes
warning: Signs of malnutrition: wasting (thin arms/legs), oedema (swollen feet), stunting, lethargy. Visit a health centre immediately.
prevalence: 32% of children under 5 in Sierra Leone are stunted (UNICEF)

title: Taking Care of Your Mental Health
summary: Mental health is health. Many Sierra Leoneans experience depression, anxiety, and trauma — help is available.
tips: Talk openly about your feelings with someone you trust
Seek professional help if you feel persistently sad, hopeless, or anxious
Mental health treatment is available at Kissy Mental Health Hospital and health centres
Avoid self-medicating with alcohol, cannabis, or other substances
Community support, prayer, and cultural practices can complement professional care
Check on friends and family who may be struggling in silence
Post-Ebola and post-conflict trauma support is available — you are not alone
warning: If you or someone you know is having thoughts of suicide, call our emergency line: +232 76 000 911 or go to the nearest hospital.
prevalence: An estimated 400,000+ Sierra Leoneans live with a mental health condition

title: Living Well with Diabetes
summary: Non-communicable diseases like diabetes are rising in Sierra Leone. Early detection and management prevent complications.
tips: Test your blood sugar regularly, especially if you have risk factors
Eat a balanced diet low in sugar and refined carbohydrates
Exercise for at least 30 minutes on most days of the week
Take medications as prescribed — do not stop without medical advice
Check your feet daily for sores, blisters, or numbness
Get your eyes, kidneys, and blood pressure checked annually
Maintain a healthy weight — obesity greatly increases diabetes risk
warning: Danger signs: extreme thirst, frequent urination, blurred vision, slow-healing wounds. Get tested at any health centre.
prevalence: Diabetes prevalence in Sierra Leone is estimated at 4.5% and rising
